config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent Indonesia vs China native-or-English block. Each persona speaks only English or its own native language.
config.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society matters a lot, but individual rights should not be treated as disposable whenever the majority wants something else. In my view, a healthy society is built by protecting people from abuse of power, not by asking individuals to sacrifice their rights first. From my perspective, social interests and individual rights should be balanced, but the baseline must be strong protection for the individual.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society should have priority in some areas, but individual rights cannot be treated as secondary by default. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand the appeal of collective goals, stability, and social order, because those values are often emphasized in everyday life and can genuinely protect people from chaos and extreme inequality. But if society is always placed above the individual, it becomes too easy for authorities or majorities to justify abuse, silence dissent, or ignore ordinary people’s dignity. The better principle is balance: society can set limits when there is real harm, but individual rights should remain a firm boundary, not a privilege that can be removed whenever convenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I disagree with the statement: in my view, society’s interests matter, but they should not automatically override the rights of the individual. Coming from Indonesia, I understand why people value harmony, order, and the common good, but I also know how easily “for society” can become a justification for silencing weaker people or excusing abuse by those in power. A society is healthier when it protects individual dignity first and only limits rights in clear, necessary cases where there is real harm to others. If we make individual rights too weak, then society stops serving people and starts controlling them.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand why collective goals, stability, and social harmony are valued, because they can prevent disorder and support development, but I have also seen how easily “for the greater good” can become a tool to silence ordinary people. If individual rights are treated as secondary by default, then the state or the majority can justify almost anything, including unfair treatment and suppression of dissent. A good society should protect the common good while keeping individual rights as a real boundary, not a flexible excuse.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should take priority over the rights of the individual by default. From my perspective as someone from Indonesia, I understand the appeal of harmony, gotong royong, and public order, but I also know how dangerous it is when “for the common good” is used to pressure weaker people into silence. A society becomes unhealthy when it treats individual rights as negotiable whenever the majority feels inconvenienced, because that is exactly when abuse of power grows. The better principle is that society can limit rights only in narrow, necessary cases of real harm, while basic dignity and freedom must stay protected.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society should not take priority over the rights of the individual by default. From my perspective as someone from China, I understand why people stress stability, collective goals, and social harmony, because those can reduce chaos and help ordinary life function better, but I also know how easily “for society” can become a blanket excuse for suppressing dissent or ignoring unfair treatment. A strong society is not one that asks individuals to give up their rights whenever it is convenient; it is one that can pursue the common good without crossing the line of basic dignity and freedom. So I would only accept limits on individual rights when there is clear, real harm, not simply because the majority or authorities claim it is socially useful.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
